agents.name: old man
agents.description: The old man is a kind and poor man who lives with his wife. He goes to the mountains to gather firewood.

agents.name: old woman
agents.description: The old woman is a kind and poor woman who lives with her husband. She goes to the stream to wash clothes.

agents.name: Momotaro
agents.description: Momotaro is the son of the old couple. He goes on a journey to get treasure from the Ogres' Island.

agents.name: monkey
agents.description: The monkey is a companion of Momotaro. He goes to the Ogres' Island with him.

agents.name: pheasant
agents.description: The pheasant is a companion of Momotaro. He flies over the castle gate to peck the Ogres.

agents.name: dog
agents.description: The dog is a companion of Momotaro. He breaks the bolts and bars with him.

agents.name: ogre
agents.description: The ogres are the enemies of Momotaro and his companions. They are overthrown by them.

events.name: Introduction to Past Time
events.description: If you’ll believe me there was a time when the fairies were none so shy as they are now. That was the time when beasts talked to men, when there were spells and enchantments and magic every day, when there was great store of hidden treasure to be dug up, and adventures for the asking.At that time, you must know, an old man and an old woman lived alone by themselves.
thoughts: The provided text appears to be a nostalgic introduction to a magical world, setting up the premise of a story that will likely explore its history and adventures. The old man and old woman mentioned at the end seem to be the main characters in this setup section.
The text describes a past time of magic and adventure, setting up the story's world and background. It appears to be an introductory or setup part of the narrative.

events.name: Elderly Couple Prepares Firewood
events.description: They were good and they were poor and they had no children at all. One fine day, “What are you doing this morning, good man?” says the old woman.“Oh,” says the old man, “I’m off to the mountains with my billhook to gather a faggot of sticks for our fire. And what are you doing, good wife?”
thoughts: The text describes a daily routine of an elderly couple, setting the scene for their life without any extraordinary events. This fits with the 'move' category as it is part of the story's setup and preparation.

The text describes a mundane daily activity of gathering firewood, which serves as a continuation of the past story's setting. It doesn't introduce conflict or significant plot progression, so it fits more into an introductory/setup phase.

events.name: Old Woman Finds Peach
events.description: “Oh,” says the old woman, “I’m off to the stream to wash clothes. It’s my washing day,” she adds.So the old man went to the mountains and the old woman went to the stream.Now, while she was washing the clothes, what should she see but a fine ripe peach that came floating down the stream? The peach was big enough, and rosy red on both sides.
thoughts: The text describes a mundane daily activity of an old woman going about her washing day routine. There's no conflict or high-stakes action yet, just a peaceful scene with the discovery of a peach floating down the stream.

events.name: She presents him the peach.
events.description: “I’m in luck this morning,” said the dame, and she pulled the peach to shore with a split bamboo stick.By-and-by, when her good man came home from the hills, she set the peach before him. “Eat, good man,” she said; “this is a lucky peach I found in the stream and brought home for you.”
thoughts: The text describes a specific action taken by the old woman, who finds and collects a peach from the stream. This is not about closure or conflict resolution, but rather an event in the story that drives it forward.

events.name: Peach Turns into Baby Suddenly
events.description: But the old man never got a taste of the peach. And why did he not?All of a sudden the peach burst in two and there was no stone to it, but a fine boy baby where the stone should have been.“Mercy me!” says the old woman.“Mercy me!” says the old man.
thoughts: The event appears to be a moment of surprise or revelation, which is not yet leading towards closure or conflict resolution. The old man and the old woman's reactions suggest a sense of wonder or astonishment rather than satisfaction or accomplishment.

The old man never gets a taste of the peach due to its unexpected transformation into a baby. This seems to be an unusual and surprising twist that doesn't quite resolve or close the story. Instead, it sets up further possibilities or raises new questions.

The given text represents an unexpected plot twist, where a peach bursts into two and turns out to be a baby instead of the expected stone. This is a surprise event that shifts the narrative's direction.

The event represents an unexpected plot twist that shifts the direction of the story. It doesn't resolve or close the story but sets up further possibilities. The old man and old woman's reactions suggest a sense of wonder and astonishment rather than satisfaction or accomplishment.
The given text represents an unexpected plot twist that surprises the characters and sets up new possibilities. This moment doesn't resolve or close the story but rather shifts its direction.

events.name: Momotaro Born from Peach
events.description: The boy baby first ate up one half of the peach and then he ate up the other half. When he had done this he was finer and stronger than ever.“Momotaro! Momotaro!” cries the old man; “the eldest son of the peach.”“Truth it is indeed,” says the old woman; “he was born in a peach.”
thoughts: The text describes a significant moment of revelation and the birth of the main character, Momotaro. This event marks a turning point in the narrative as it sets the stage for the boy's growth and future adventures.

events.name: Momotaro Becomes Young Man
events.description: Both of them took such good care of Momotaro that soon he was the stoutest and bravest boy of all that country-side. He was a credit to them, you may believe. The neighbours nodded their heads and they said, “Momotaro is the fine young man!”
thoughts: The given text is part of a past story and shows the development and growth of Momotaro as a child. The neighbors' recognition of him as the fine young man indicates a turning point in his life where he has become a notable figure in his community.

events.name: Momotaro Requests Kimi-Dango
events.description: “Mother,” says Momotaro one day to the old woman, “make me a good store of kimi-dango” (which is the way that they call millet dumplings in those parts).“What for do you want kimi-dango?” says his mother.

events.name: Momotaro Prepares Departure
events.description: “Why,” says Momotaro, “I’m going on a journey, or as you may say, an adventure, and I shall be needing the kimi-dango on the way.”“Where are you going, Momotaro?” says his mother.“I’m off to the Ogres’ Island,” says Momotaro, “to get their treasure, and I should be obliged if you’d let me have the kimi-dango as soon as may be,” he says.
thoughts: The text describes Momotaro preparing for a journey to the Ogres' Island to retrieve their treasure. events.name: Momotaro Prepares to Depart
events.description: So they made him the kimi-dango, and he put them in a wallet, and he tied the wallet to his girdle and off he set.“Sayonara, and good luck to you, Momotaro!” cried the old man and the old woman.“Sayonara! Sayonara!” cried Momotaro.
thoughts: The event involves Momotaro preparing for his journey by making and storing kimi-dango, which is a pivotal moment in the story as he sets out to get the Ogres' treasure. This can be classified as part of the setup or preparation phase before the main conflict.

cried 'Sayonara, and good luck to you, Momotaro!'

events.name: Momotaro Meets Monkey Ally
events.description: He hadn’t gone far when he fell in with a monkey.“Kia! Kia!” says the monkey. “Where are you off to, Momotaro?”Says Momotaro, “I’m off to the Ogres’ Island for an adventure.”
thoughts: The text describes an encounter between Momotaro and a monkey as he prepares for his journey to the Ogres' Island. This scene is part of the preparation or setup phase, where the protagonist is gathering information and receiving help for his adventure.

events.description: “Give me one,” says the monkey, “and I will go with you.”So Momotaro gave a millet dumpling to the monkey, and the two of them jogged on together. They hadn’t gone far when they fell in with a pheasant.

events.name: Momotaro Joins Dog Ally
events.description: They hadn’t gone far when they fell in with a dog.“Bow! Wow! Wow!” says the dog. “Where are you off to, Momotaro?”Says Momotaro, “I’m off to the Ogres’ Island.”“What have you got in your wallet, Momotaro?”

events.name: Momotaro gives dog a dumpling.
events.description: “I’ve got some of the best millet dumplings in all Japan.”“Give me one,” says the dog, “and I will go with you.”So Momotaro gave a millet dumpling to the dog, and the four of them jogged on together.

events.name: Momotaro Presents Invasion Plan
events.description: By-and-by they came to the Ogres’ Island.“Now, brothers,” says Momotaro, “listen to my plan. The pheasant must fly over the castle gate and peck the Ogres. The monkey must climb over the castle wall and pinch the Ogres. The dog and I will break the bolts and bars.

events.description: Momotaro broke the bolts and bars, and the dog leapt into the castle courtyard. “Bow! Wow! Wow!”The brave companions fought till sundown and overcame the Ogres.

events.name: Momotaro Takes Ogre Treasure
events.description: Those that were left alive they took prisoners and bound with cords—a wicked lot they were.“Now, brothers,” says Momotaro, “bring out the Ogres’ treasure.”So they did.The treasure was worth having, indeed.
thoughts: This event marks a key turning point in Momotaro's adventure, where he and his companions successfully overcome the Ogres. The outcome of this battle will likely have a significant impact on the rest of the narrative.

events.name: Momotaro Offers Riches
events.description: There were magic jewels there, and caps and coats to make you invisible. There was gold and silver, and jade and coral, and amber and tortoise-shell and mother-of-pearl.“Here’s riches for all,” says Momotaro. “Choose, brothers, and take your fill.”

events.name: Momotaro Claims Treasure
events.description: “Kia! Kia!” says the monkey. “Thanks, my Lord Momotaro.”“Ken! Ken!” says the pheasant. “Thanks, my Lord Momotaro.”“Bow! Wow! Wow!” says the dog. “Thanks, my dear Lord Momotaro.”
